cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

migration.notes: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish, Woodbury. One of the leading men of the town for many years.

narrative_blocks.header: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1720 he was among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish in Woodbury, Connecticut. He was described as 'for many years one of the leading men of the town.'

narrative_blocks.header: Died suddenly in 1756
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's record says he 'died suddenly' in February 1756, at age sixty-seven. The unexpected death cut short his role as one of the leading men of Bethlehem Parish — a settlement he had helped establish thirty-five years before.

bio.notes: Also spelled Curtiss in some records

burial.plot_notes: Ward Cemetery, Poultney, Rutland County, Vermont
Old burial ground near Bristol-Plainville line, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: His sons turned the farm into factories
narrative_blocks.body: Asahel kept a large farm near the Bristol-Plainville line from about 1760, and with his sons took up manufacturing beside it — the seed of three generations of Hooker mills. He was buried in the old ground near the line he farmed.

burial.plot_notes: Old Westmoreland Cemetery, Westmoreland, Oneida County, New York

cross_connections.display_label: His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

parents.father_research_notes: EH says father was Deacon Thomas Hart — WRONG. Barbour Collection Farmington VR confirms father was Dea. Ebenezer Hart (X00119). Primary source wins.

sources.notes: Barbour Collection confirms: father = Dea. Ebenezer Hart

bio.notes: EH gives 'Mercy (Mary) Hart' — both names used interchangeably

ministry_positions.notes: Ordained Dec 5, 1753; settled there 24 years until death by smallpox

primary_residence.notes: Minister here 24 years 1753-1777. Yale 1751, ordained Dec 5, 1753.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Jonathan Edwards' Northampton pulpit, died of smallpox
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained in 1753, he became pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts — the church where Jonathan Edwards had preached until his dismissal in 1750. He held that pulpit for nearly 25 years, dying of smallpox on February 6, 1777, in his 48th year. His was the most prominent pastoral appointment held by any Hooker descendant of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's father, Colonel Worthington, named Worthington Parish
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sarah Worthington, daughter of Colonel Worthington of Springfield, Massachusetts — the man who gave his name to Worthington Parish in Berlin, Connecticut, the parish where the Hart and Hooker families had long been prominent. The marriage connected the Hooker line to one of western Massachusetts's most distinguished families.

narrative_blocks.header: Smallpox took the Northampton pastor at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: He served as pastor of the Northampton MA Congregational Church from 1753 until smallpox killed him in February 1777 at forty-seven. His daughter Sarah Hooker married the Northampton lawyer Caleb Strong nine months later — passing the Hooker name into the family of a future Massachusetts Governor and U.S. Senator.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards's daughter refused his pulpit and her chair sat in the vestibule.

primary_residence.notes: Seth Hooker of Kensington — hatter and farmer on the Gay place at Hinsdale NH

death.cause: Year only; month/day approximate per project convention. Source: 'died 1761 aged 63.'

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons served the Revolution differently
narrative_blocks.body: Her eldest son Selah rose to General and was captured by the British in 1776; Noadiah served as Lieutenant; and Asahel, Yale-educated and ordained as a minister, died in 1775 at age 33 before the war's outcome was known. Three brothers from one Hooker-Hart household, three paths through the Revolution.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Lieut. Samuel Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Mary Hooker (H00100) in 1723; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Matthew Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101) in 1726; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; Hartford founding Hart family

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. John Hart (b. Apr 2, 1655, d. Nov 11, 1714)

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Nathaniel Hart married I00028's wife's sister Mary Hooker (H00100, b.~1699); she had married Lieut. Samuel Hart in 1723.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Nathaniel Hart married I00028's wife's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101, b.~1701); she had married Matthew Hart in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: Nathaniel died young
narrative_blocks.body: Nathaniel died in 1728, in the first year. Nathaniel’s parents gave the name to a child born seven years later.

sources.short: Slavery in Berlin — Forgotten No Longer

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin, Hartford County, Connecticut

H00273.prominent_name_reason: General Selah Hart (1732-1806) — Brigadier General, Connecticut Militia. Lt. Col. 15th Regiment under Col. Fisher Gay; captured at Battle of Long Island Aug 27, 1776. Held prisoner ~2 years while wife Ruth knew not if he lived. Promoted to Colonel in captivity after Gay's death. Appointed Brigadier General, 6th Brigade of Militia 1779. Delegate to State Convention for ratification of US Constitution, 1788. Post-war: magistrate, constable, deacon, treasurer, general of CT Militia for many years. "Patron Saint of Connecticut" per later sources. Monument in Farmington Cemetery. Slave owner: Jack and Pharaoh Hart. Pharaoh served at Germantown (1777) and Monmouth (1778); Hart demanded all of Pharaoh's army wages as compensation for freeing him. Jack freed voluntarily at 21 without payment. See: https://digitalfarmington.org/digital-farmington-project/selah-hart-saint-and-sinner/

political_offices.office: Delegate, CT State Convention for US Constitution Ratification

enslaved_persons.name: Jack
enslaved_persons.description: Taken as a very young child. Freed voluntarily at age 21 without payment. Hart told him: "The law would give me four years more of your service, but I think you have the right to be free." Received education and clothing better than average white lads in the vicinity.
enslaved_persons.source: History of New Britain; Berlin slavery article 2022

enslaved_persons.name: Pharaoh
enslaved_persons.description: Allowed to enlist in Continental Army for 3 years on condition of paying Hart a portion of wages for freedom. Fought at Battle of Germantown PA (1777) and Monmouth NJ (1778). At end of service, Hart claimed ALL wages — not just a portion — citing his own imprisonment debt. Pharaoh had no choice but to pay. 35 years later, both Pharaoh and Selah Hart received pensions. In 1777, Selah sat in a British prison while Pharaoh fought for American freedom.
enslaved_persons.source: Digital Farmington / Menapace 2022; Berlin slavery article

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary brigadier general, prisoner of war, slaveholder

cross_connections.display_label: Managed this mine for the Revolution — Percival surveyed it as geologist
First cousins — both commanded Connecticut militia in the Revolution.
Adopted nephew who inherited the General Hart homestead
cross_connections.notes: Cyprian Hart (H00562, son of Selah's brother Nathaniel Hart No. 2) was orphaned of his father at age 4 in 1773. The childless Selah adopted him as heir and raised him at the Kensington homestead. Cyprian m. Lucy Hooker (H00537) Dec 24, 1795 and died fourteen weeks before his uncle on March 1, 1806. His widow Lucy and Selah's widow Ruth Cole Hart kept the General Hart house together for 38 years.

cross_connections.display_label: Great-niece whose marriage carried the homestead out of Hart hands
cross_connections.notes: Abigail Hart (H01446, daughter of his adopted nephew Cyprian Hart) m. Oliver Moore March 25, 1829. Per Hart Genealogy, the former General Hart homestead became the Moore residence by the time of Abigail's death in 1850 — three-generation property descent ending the Hart family ownership: Gen. Selah Hart → adopted nephew Cyprian Hart → daughter Abigail → husband Oliver Moore.

cross_connections.display_label: served in his Revolutionary militia company
served in his company guarding Boston in early 1776

narrative_blocks.header: Captured retreating from New York — held two years
narrative_blocks.body: He was captured on September 15, 1776 while retreating from New York City, and held prisoner for approximately two years. His enslaved man Pharaoh was allowed to enlist and earn his freedom in his absence — Pharaoh fought at Germantown in 1777 and at Monmouth in 1778; thirty-five years later both Hart and Pharaoh received federal pensions.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington breakfasted with him
narrative_blocks.body: On November 10, 1789, President Washington breakfasted in Berlin while touring Connecticut. Hart greeted him and later recorded that he was 'too full for utterance.' His wife Ruth Cole lived to approximately age 101, outliving him by 38 years.

narrative_blocks.header: He freed enslaved Jack four years early
narrative_blocks.body: "The law would give me four years more of your service, but I think you have the right to be free," he told Jack. He also gave Pharaoh the chance to earn freedom through military service — an unusual act for a Connecticut officer of his era.

narrative_blocks.header: He took every shilling a freedman earned
narrative_blocks.body: Selah promised Pharaoh freedom for three years in the Continental Army while collecting part of his wages as compensation. When the term ended, Selah — in debt from his imprisonment — demanded the entirety. Pharaoh, who had fought at Germantown and Monmouth for a freedom he had never known, had no choice but to pay.

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant while his brother Selah was a General
narrative_blocks.detail: The Hart family of Kensington had deep military roots: their father Nathaniel Hart (I00028) had married Abigail Hooker in 1719 in one of the three simultaneous Hart-Hooker marriages. A third brother, Rev. Asahel Hart (H00276, Yale 1764), became the first pastor of North Canaan Congregational Church — the three Hart brothers who survived to adulthood representing military service, Congregationalist ministry, and civic life respectively. Noadiah was appointed administrator of the estate of Jonathan Hurlbut Jr. in October 1761, confirming his standing in the Kensington community. (EH 1909; Hart Genealogy 1948)
narrative_blocks.body: Noadiah Hart served as a Lieutenant in the American Revolutionary War while his older brother Selah Hart rose to General in the Connecticut Militia — captured by the British in September 1776 during the New York retreat. Both were sons of Nathaniel Hart × Abigail Hooker, 4th-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker. Noadiah served through the war, became a Deacon in the Harwinton church by 1804, and died in Southington in 1817 at 80.

narrative_blocks.header: Deacon of Harwinton church; lived to 80
narrative_blocks.body: After the war, he became Deacon of the church in Harwinton in 1804, removed to Southington, and lived in his daughter Velina's household near her mill — a site later covered by the "Plant Ponds." He died in 1817 at age 80, the longest-lived of the three brothers.

church_roles.location: Second Church of Christ, North Canaan, Connecticut
church_roles.notes: Died in 6th year of ministry, age 33

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1764 — first pastor of North Canaan
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1764, organized the North Canaan Congregational Church on December 5, 1769, and was installed as its first pastor on March 14, 1770. He died unmarried on June 28, 1775 — at 33 years old, just as the Revolutionary War was beginning, two of his brothers about to serve.

open_questions.question: Elihu Burritt descent through this line
open_questions.notes: CLOSED: Elihu Burritt's Hooker descent through this line is NOT CONFIRMED. His connection to the tree is through KINSHIP, not descent: his grandfather Elijah Hinsdale (X01931) and Lydia Hinsdale Hart (I00034) were siblings — both children of Capt. John Hinsdale (X00039). This makes Burritt the first cousin once removed of Emma Hart Willard (H00597) and Almira Hart Phelps (H00598). The claim that Burritt descended from Thomas Hooker through Huldah Hart (H00280) could not be traced through available sources. He is documented in the tree as an orbit_notable (X01932) with the Hinsdale kinship connection noted.

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Nathaniel Hart married Mary Hooker's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099) in 1719; Mary Hooker married I00029 in 1723.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Matthew Hart married Mary Hooker's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101) in 1726; Mary Hooker married I00029 in 1723.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Lieut. Samuel Hart married I00029's wife's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099, b.~1697); she had married Nathaniel Hart in 1719.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Lieut. Samuel Hart married I00029's wife's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101, b.~1701); she had married Matthew Hart in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: Died when his only son was fourteen
narrative_blocks.body: Lieut. Samuel Hart had planned a collegiate education for his only son Samuel, but died when the boy was about fourteen. His widow, unwilling to part with him, kept him home and provided the best education locally available.

burial.plot_notes: Maple Cemetery, Berlin, Hartford County, Connecticut. 41.61167, -72.74837. Buried next to parents Samuel and Mary Hooker Hart. Rachel (Porter) Hollister buried on the other side. Ephraim buried apart from his wives.

cross_connections.display_label: Her uncle who became guardian of her son Abel Hollister

narrative_blocks.header: Married three times — both Hollisters second cousins
narrative_blocks.body: Her first husband Thomas Hollister of Kensington died 1748 leaving her a young widow. Her second husband Josiah Beckley died 1757; her third husband Capt. Ephraim Hollister was Thomas's second cousin, returning her to the same Hollister family.

narrative_blocks.header: Uncle John Hooker became guardian of her son
narrative_blocks.body: After her first husband died in 1748, her father Lieut. Samuel Hart Sr. was appointed guardian of her young son Abel Hollister. When Samuel Hart died, her uncle John Hooker assumed Abel's guardianship — bringing the boy under direct Hooker family care.

narrative_blocks.header: Third husband buried apart from his wives
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. Ephraim Hollister outlived her by four years, dying March 3, 1804 in Berlin, Connecticut, age 79. He was buried in Maple Cemetery — apart from both his wives.

narrative_blocks.header: Asahel died young
narrative_blocks.body: Asahel died in 1736 at the age of nine.

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin, Hartford County, Connecticut.

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband descended from him — Hartford-founder peer of Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Welles family of Wethersfield
narrative_blocks.body: In 1751 she married Dr. Joseph Welles of Wethersfield — a physician of considerable note and extensive practice in Kensington. His Welles family descended from Gov. Thomas Welles, 4th Governor of Connecticut Colony and a Hartford founder alongside Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage united two Hartford founding families
narrative_blocks.body: Her marriage joined the Hooker line (through her great-grandfather Samuel Hooker) and the Welles line (through her husband's descent from Gov. Thomas Welles). Both Thomas Hooker and Thomas Welles arrived in Hartford in 1636, building the Connecticut Colony together.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousins and double-cousins filled her childhood
narrative_blocks.body: Three Hart brothers married three Hooker sisters in Farmington between 1719 and 1725. Their children — including her — were each other's first cousins on both sides.

narrative_blocks.header: Died the same day her widower remarried
narrative_blocks.body: She died on December 24, 1778 in Kensington at age forty-seven. Her husband Gideon Porter married Hannah, the widow of Avery Heath, that same day.

sources.short: Hart Article (Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps, NYGB Record)
sources.notes: Written by H00598 Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps, age ~91, published in New York Genealogical and Biographical Record. Full text preserved in EH entry notes.

sources.short: Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart (1875)
Revolutionary Soldiers of the Town of Berlin (Berlin-Peck Memorial Library)

narrative_blocks.header: Wounded at Saratoga, September 1777
narrative_blocks.body: Serving as a lieutenant in General Wolcott's detachment, Samuel Hart was wounded at Saratoga on September 19, 1777, and two years later led the Berlin company that turned out at the New Haven alarm.

narrative_blocks.header: A thinker who could not speak unwritten
narrative_blocks.body: A great reader, he could not address an audience without first writing out his ideas; in religion he turned Universalist after a church dispute.

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary captain, father of Emma Hart Willard

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both commanded Connecticut militia in the Revolution.

documents.document_blurb: Emma Willard's 1855 poem names both parents — the most direct portrait of Samuel Hart's character.
Almira Hart Phelps article on the Hart family, NYGB Record.

military_service.unit: Col. Cook's Regiment; Berlin militia company
military_service.notes: Lieutenant under Gen. Wolcott's detachment at Saratoga, wounded 19 Sep 1777; captain of the Berlin company at the New Haven alarm, 1779, repelling Tryon's invasion.
Fifteen days, 8-22 Aug 1757, in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry.

career.role: First Clerk and Treasurer, Berlin Congregational Church
career.notes: Withdrew from the Second Congregational Church in 1807 after a religious dispute.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Nathaniel Hart married Sarah Hooker's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099) in 1719; Sarah Hooker married I00030 in 1726.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Lieut. Samuel Hart married Sarah Hooker's sister Mary Hooker (H00100) in 1723; Sarah Hooker married I00030 in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

death.cause: Died before posthumous son Matthew (H00286) was born Jan 23, 1737

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. John Hart (b. Apr 2, 1655, d. Nov 11, 1714)

burial.plot_notes: Christian Lane Cemetery, Kensington, Connecticut — same cemetery as Samuel Hooker (H00086)

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Matthew Hart married I00030's wife's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099, b.~1697); she had married Nathaniel Hart in 1719.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Matthew Hart married I00030's wife's sister Mary Hooker (H00100, b.~1699); she had married Lieut. Samuel Hart in 1723.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Stebbins (m. Nov 17, 1683; dau. of John Stebbins and Abigail Bartlett)
parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Strong Jr. (b. Nov 16, 1661 Northampton; d. Nov 30, 1735) — son of Thomas Strong Sr. and Mary Huit (whose surname "Huit" this child carried as a given name)

burial.plot_notes: South Burying Ground, Kensington, Hartford County, Connecticut.

cross_connections.display_label: His son Matthew Hart Jr. married Urania Hooker — both.
His son Matthew Hart Jr. married Urania Hooker — both.
commanded his Revolutionary militia company

military_service.notes: Served under Capts. Selah Hart and James Stoddard, Col. Wolcott's regiment.

narrative_blocks.header: Born three months after his father died
narrative_blocks.body: His father Matthew Hart died on October 30, 1736; he was born January 23, 1737 — a posthumous child. He grew up in Kensington and joined the church there in October 1760 at age twenty-three.

narrative_blocks.header: Son's marriage created double Hooker descent
narrative_blocks.body: His son Matthew Hart III married Urania Hooker — both Hooker descendants. Their grandchildren carried double descent from Thomas Hooker through different family lines.

narrative_blocks.header: Removed his household to New York around 1800
narrative_blocks.body: Around the turn of the nineteenth century he sold the Sheldon Moore homestead in Kensington and moved to Madison County, New York — to either Whitestown or De Ruyter. He died there around 1811, more than a decade after the move.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson became America's foremost 1820s poet
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson James Gates Percival (1795-1856) — son of daughter Elizabeth and Dr. James Percival Jr. — became the most recognized American poet of the 1820s and Connecticut's first state geologist. James was born in Kensington before Matthew Hart Jr. departed for New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Born after his father had died
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Hart was born in January 1737, weeks after his father died the previous October. He grew up to serve as a Revolutionary corporal and later removed to New York.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery (Ancient Burying Ground), Farmington, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: Farmington militia captain his entire life
narrative_blocks.body: He held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut militia and was known by that title for the rest of his life. He spent his entire life in Farmington — born there February 15, 1704/5 and dying there December 19, 1764, in his 60th year.

narrative_blocks.header: The big chair — militia saluted him sitting
narrative_blocks.body: In later years he became so corpulent he could not attend to business. On fine days a large chair was placed in the front yard near the door, where he sat smoking his pipe and visiting with passersby. When the local militia paraded, it was customary for them to march around by his house and salute him in the big chair.

narrative_blocks.header: Heir to the famous Farmington Hooker Elm
narrative_blocks.body: He was the son of Hon. John Hooker — the Farmington judge whose famous Hooker Elm gave the town its hospitality tradition. His grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker had ministered Farmington's church for thirty years; his great-great-grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker founded Hartford.

sources.short: Connecticut Town Marriage Records pre-1870 (Barbour Collection)
Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT

burial.plot_notes: Find a Grave memorial — Memento Mori Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Her second cousin Martha married her husband's older brother Ezekiel.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-six leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail married James E. Cowles in August 1753 — same Farmington family network that produced her cousin Martha's marriage to James's older brother Ezekiel a decade earlier. She bore seven children between 1754 and 1771 and died at thirty-six in February 1772, eighteen months before her husband remarried Mary Bull.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her cousin married Cowles brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail and her second cousin Martha Hooker Cowles married brothers — Abigail to James E. Cowles in 1753, Martha to Ezekiel Cowles a decade earlier. Both Hooker cousins descended from Rev. Samuel Hooker through different sons; both Cowles brothers were sons of Capt. Isaac Cowles by his third wife Elizabeth Smith.

H00288.prominent_name_reason: Col. Noadiah Hooker — Revolutionary War officer who raised the first Farmington troops and commanded a regiment at Burgoyne's surrender (Saratoga, 1777); 40 years Justice of the Peace; Farmington representative many sessions. "In all relations of life, both public and private, he discharged every duty with credit to himself and benefit to his fellow citizens."

cross_connections.display_label: Bull engraved the Farmington Library bookplate; both on founding committee.
First cousins who served together — Roger enlisted in his company.
Friend-compatriots in the Revolutionary War.
Norton served under his command at Peekskill in spring 1777.

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary War Colonel; 40 years Justice of Peace

narrative_blocks.header: Commanded a regiment at Burgoyne's surrender
narrative_blocks.body: His Connecticut regiment fought in the Saratoga Campaign of 1777, where John Burgoyne's British army laid down its arms. The victory turned the war and brought France in on the American side.

narrative_blocks.header: First Farmington troops to reach Boston
narrative_blocks.body: He raised a hundred-man company of Farmington's elite and arrived at Roxbury three weeks before any other Connecticut soldiers. The Colonial Assembly commissioned him captain on arrival.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent an Oneida boy to mission school
narrative_blocks.body: In 1819 he paid for the education of eighteen-year-old Peter Augustus Takonotas at the Cornwall Foreign Mission School. The boy later took Hooker's surname.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace for forty years
narrative_blocks.body: After the war he served as Farmington's Justice of the Peace from the 1780s through the 1820s. The role made him the local arbiter of debt, marriage, and civil disputes for two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Late returning a library book in 1795
narrative_blocks.body: The Farmington Monthly Library, founded that year, recorded 'Col. Noadiah Hooker his shilling' for a late-return fine. Julius Gay's nineteenth-century history pamphlets preserved the entry.

education.school_name: No college noted — rose through militia ranks

military_service.notes: Raised the first troops enlisted at Farmington and marched in command of a company of 100+ men — the very elite of the vicinity. Arrived in Boston (Roxbury) three weeks before any other enlisted men from Connecticut. Commissioned Captain by Colonial Assembly. Commissioned Colonel by State Legislature 1776. Served in several regiments until close of War. Had command of a regiment at the capture of Burgoyne's army (Saratoga, 1777). Facebook genealogy research (2024) confirms: Samuel Woodruff (1734-1816) of Farmington CT served as private in Capt. Jesse Curtiss' company, Col. Noadiah Hooker's regiment, 1777. Also confirmed by Orderly Book of Capt. Ichabod Norton (1777, Peekskill): Norton served "under Colonel Hooker" at Peekskill 1777. Norton was husband of Ruth Strong (H00297), daughter of Ruth Hooker Strong — a Hooker descendant herself. Two branches of the Hooker tree serving together.
military_service.unit: Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment, Connecticut Militia

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut

military_service.notes: Lieutenant in the Militia pre-Revolution. Remained neutral during the war through the influence of his Loyalist wife's family.
Active in Farmington town affairs before the war; remained neutral through it (EH #292).

narrative_blocks.header: Neutral in the Revolution, ostracized afterward
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Ingersoll's family were Loyalists; Joseph remained neutral throughout the war. Afterward, while his brother and cousins who had served received honors, Joseph found 'the confidence of the town was entirely withheld from him.'

narrative_blocks.header: Married into a Loyalist Hooker descendant family
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Ingersoll was the daughter of Rev. Jonathan Ingersoll — himself a Hooker descendant through a separate line. The Ingersoll family were committed Loyalists; this marriage united two Hooker-descendant branches across opposing political loyalties.

narrative_blocks.header: Rebuilt his reputation in Litchfield, New York
narrative_blocks.body: He removed to Litchfield in Herkimer County, New York, where he became active and trusted in public affairs. His new neighbors honored his integrity and loyalty to the new nation, restoring the standing he had lost in Farmington.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker women who married Strongs in 1729 and 1813

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children with Capt. Asahel Strong Jr.
narrative_blocks.body: She married Capt. Asahel Strong Jr. on January 8, 1729 and bore him eight children — including Rev. Cyprian Strong of Chatham. Asahel was a grandson of Stephen Hart through his mother Margaret Hart, closing another loop in the Hart-Hooker network.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost three children in September 1736
narrative_blocks.body: In a single ten-day span — September 6 to 15, 1736 — three of her young children died: Lois and Ruth on the same day, then Elnathan nine days later. The cluster strongly suggests an epidemic moving through Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: Second husband Solomon Whitman lived to 93
narrative_blocks.body: After Capt. Asahel Strong's death she married Solomon Whitman, who was Town Clerk at 92 and made a pair of shoes on his 90th birthday. He died at 93 from over-fatigue settling a fence dispute between two brothers.

parents.mother_research_notes: Margaret Hart — daughter of Capt. Thomas Hart (X00031), son of Stephen Hart (X00034)
parents.father_research_notes: Asahel Strong Sr. (b. Nov 14, 1668 Northampton MA) — married Margaret Hart, daughter of Capt. Thomas Hart (X00031)

cross_connections.display_label: Both Strong men who married Hooker descendants, 84 years apart

narrative_blocks.header: His Hooker marriage echoed down two more generations
narrative_blocks.body: When Captain Asahel Strong Jr. married Sarah Hooker in 1729 at Farmington, Connecticut — a great-great-granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker — he began a pattern that would echo through generations: their great-great-grandson Asahel Hooker Strong was born in 1776 with 'Hooker' preserved as his middle name, and 84 years after the first marriage, Judge Elisha Beebe Strong married another Thomas Hooker descendant, Dolly Goodwin Hooker, at Canandaigua, New York in 1813. By 1866, that second union had produced Beebe Hooker Strong of Milwaukee, the fourth person in the Strong family to carry the Hooker name — connecting the original Farmington parsonage to the Wisconsin frontier across 137 years.

political_offices.title: First Judge, Probate Court of the Farmington District

narrative_blocks.header: Celebrated his 90th birthday by making a pair
narrative_blocks.body: Solomon Whitman had learned shoemaking as a young man but rarely practiced the trade professionally. At 90, he made a pair of shoes — apparently for the occasion. Though never a college graduate, he was described as well-educated and deeply interested in literary matters.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at 93
narrative_blocks.body: Solomon Whitman's death at age 93 was said to have been caused by over-fatigue sustained while settling a fence boundary dispute between two brothers. He died September 13, 1803 in Farmington, Connecticut.

sources.short: Orderly Book of Capt. Ichabod Norton
sources.notes: Original MS owned by Charles Mulford Robinson, Rochester NY, 1909.

military_service.notes: Ruth Strong Norton — wife of Col. Ichabod Norton.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband served under her Hooker cousin in 1777
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Major Ichabod Norton entered the Revolutionary army as a lieutenant in 1776, fighting at Skeensborough, Fort Ann, and Ticonderoga. In 1777 at Peekskill he served under Colonel Noadiah Hooker — Ruth's first cousin once removed in the Hooker line — and was eventually promoted to Major.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Ruths — only one survived past four
narrative_blocks.body: Her parents' first daughter Ruth had died at age four on September 6, 1736; she was born three years later in 1739 and given the same name. Replacement naming was a common Puritan practice when an infant died young, but it left the second Ruth carrying her dead sister's identity for life.

burial.plot_notes: Gravestone: "Rev. Cyprian Strong, D.D. Died Nov. 17th. 1811 In the 68th year of his age, And 44th of his ministry; In morals, exemplary; In doctrine, uncorrupt; In reasoning, profound; In declaring Gods counsel, Perspicuous and solemn; And in death, peaceful."

narrative_blocks.header: Two siblings married two siblings — one ceremony
narrative_blocks.body: On November 9, 1768 in Farmington, Cyprian Strong married Sarah Bull, and his sister Elizabeth Strong married Sarah's brother Martin Bull — both weddings on the same day. Two Strong siblings and two Bull siblings, bound together in a single ceremony.

narrative_blocks.header: His gravestone counts 44 years of ministry
narrative_blocks.body: He was ordained at Chatham (now Portland) on August 9, 1767 and remained its Congregational pastor until his death on November 17, 1811 — 44 years in the same pulpit. His gravestone records both: "In the 68th year of his age, And 44th of his ministry; In morals, exemplary; In doctrine, uncorrupt; In reasoning, profound."

narrative_blocks.header: Yale or Harvard? — the record says Yale
narrative_blocks.body: EH, the Strong family book, and Yale archival records all confirm he graduated Yale 1763. The Native Northeast Portal biography states Harvard — this appears to be an error in that source. He served as Farmington Town Clerk 1764-67 immediately after graduation, before ordination.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter walked the Trail of Tears
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Sarah Strong married Judge John Ellsworth; their daughter Harriet Ellsworth married Ainsworth Blunt at Brainerd Mission, Tennessee, where they ministered to the Cherokees. When the forced removal came in 1838, Blunt accompanied the Cherokees on the Trail of Tears before illness forced him back from the Mississippi. He later became the first mayor of Dalton, Georgia.

education.notes: EH, Strong family book (5345-8), and Yale archival collection all confirm Yale 1763. Native Northeast Portal biography states Harvard — this appears to be an error in that source. Sprague Annals of the American Pulpit vol. I pp. 651-3 contains a life sketch.

burial.plot_notes: Buried with husband Martin Bull.

narrative_blocks.header: Two siblings married two siblings — one ceremony
narrative_blocks.body: On November 9, 1768 in Farmington, Cyprian Strong married Sarah Bull, and his sister Elizabeth Strong married Sarah's brother Martin Bull — both weddings on the same day. Two Strong siblings and two Bull siblings, bound together in a single ceremony.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the Hooker Elm's famous hospitality alive
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited the Farmington estate and the famous Hooker Elm from his father John. EH described him as 'a thorough gentleman of the old school' who continued the famed hospitality of the elm gatherings.

narrative_blocks.header: Favorite son and keeper of family history
narrative_blocks.body: He accompanied his father John on official journeys around the Colony and brought his first wife to live in the family house. EH wrote that he received from his father full information about the family's English origins — and that his father was the best informed of all Thomas Hooker's grandsons on the subject.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sisters married Hart brothers — knot tied
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah each married a son of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. Through those three marriages, Roger found himself simultaneously brother-in-law to Nathaniel, Samuel, and Matthew Hart — the Hart and Hooker families fully intertwined.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His son Thomas Hooker fathered Abigail Hooker, who married Samuel Talcott of Hartford. The marriage merged the Hooker surname into the Talcott line — a transition that defines several gen 6+ branches.

parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Bull — daughter of Thomas Bull, a Hartford Founder
parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Josiah Hart of Farmington — son of John Hart, grandson of Stephen Hart (X00034)
Capt. Martin Kellogg of Newington, Connecticut

cross_connections.display_label: Her great-great-great-granddaughter mapped Cepheid period-luminosity
Her descendant designed USS Hartford's steam engine
Her grandson sheltered fugitive Basil Dorsey five years

bio.notes: Sarah No. 2 — replacement naming after first Sarah (H00301) who died April 1741

narrative_blocks.header: Married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt of Heath
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt and moved to Heath, Massachusetts. Leavitt was twice dismissed from churches — once for dragging a runaway enslaved woman home by rope, and once for Loyalist sympathies.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children — eleven sons and one daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She had twelve children with Jonathan Leavitt — eleven sons and one daughter named Clarissa. EH lists only three; other sources confirm the full count of twelve.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter gave her wrong medicine, killing her
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly in 1791 after her daughter Clarissa gave her the wrong medicine. Clarissa herself died unmarried seven years later — never recovering from the accident.

narrative_blocks.header: Underground Railroad station owners descended from her
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her grandsons — Roger Hooker Leavitt and Hart Leavitt — operated Underground Railroad stations in Charlemont, Massachusetts, sheltering the fugitive Basil Dorsey for five years. Roger's home is on the National Park Service Underground Railroad Network to Freedom.

narrative_blocks.header: A wrong medicine, given by her daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly at Heath on October 11, 1791, after taking the wrong medicine — handed to her, the Yale record says, by her daughter Clarissa. The funeral text was “He hath done all things well”; the Connecticut Courant carried the notice.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Held British Loyalist prisoners here during the Revolution.

notable.notable_blurb: Freed enslaved Bristow before enlisting; Siege of Boston

narrative_blocks.header: Died of pleurisy at Boston — unmarked grave
narrative_blocks.body: He was among the first to enlist at Farmington in 1775 and joined the army near Boston. He died of pleurisy on November 26, 1775, age 30, during the Siege of Boston — buried in an unmarked grave at Roxbury. EH wrote: "Today the ashes of those patriot dead rest peacefully beneath the wild turmoil of Boston's streets."

narrative_blocks.header: Freed Bristow days before enlisting — £60 paid
narrative_blocks.body: On May 9, 1775 — days before leaving for war — he signed a Deed of Manumission freeing Bristow, an African man age 44, who paid £60 (roughly 400 days' wages) for his freedom. The deed reads: "he may go & come when & wherever he pleases." Bristow later willed his estate to Thomas's children. A West Hartford middle school was named Bristow Middle School in 2005 — believed to be the first Connecticut school named for a formerly enslaved person.

narrative_blocks.header: Half-brother Roger survived — rose to Major
narrative_blocks.body: He and his half-brother Roger Hooker — different mothers; Roger's was Anna Kellogg, his was Mercy Hart — both answered the Revolution's first call. Thomas died of disease in 1775; Roger rose to Major, carried news of Burgoyne's surrender to the British, and outlived the war by 55 years.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Abigail Pantry Hooker married Samuel Talcott, uniting the Hooker and Talcott founding-family lines of Hartford. This is the compiler's primary point of double descent from Thomas Hooker and the Talcott family simultaneously.

narrative_blocks.header: West Hartford home now a National Register site
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hart built his West Hartford home in 1770 — the Sarah Whitman Hooker Homestead at the corner of South Main Street and New Britain Avenue, where his widow Sarah continued to live and raise their children after his death. The house stands on the National Register of Historic Places, named for her.

documents.document_blurb: Summons to appear before CT General Assembly, Woodruff v. Samuel Hooker.

military_service.unit: Suffolk County Militia, Massachusetts Militia; Connecticut volunteers from Farmington
military_service.notes: Enlisted at first call 1775. "One of the first to enlist." Joined army near Boston; died of pleurisy Nov 26, 1775 during Siege of Boston. Buried in unmarked grave at Roxbury MA.

bio.notes: Middle name "Hart" commemorates his mother Mercy Hart
Named for maternal grandfather Capt. Martin Kellogg

narrative_blocks.header: He and Roger bought 40 acres from Tunxis
narrative_blocks.body: In 1776, he and his brother Roger purchased 40 acres at Congee, Farmington, including a dwelling house and barn, from Tunxis men Cornelius Indian and Elijah Wampey for £150. The deed is among the documented transfers of Native land in the Farmington area, preserved at the Native Northeast Portal.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Georgian Colonial; family home for three Hooker generations.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins who served together — Roger enlisted in Noadiah's company.
First cousins once removed — both married into the Treadwell family.

notable.notable_blurb: Continental Army Major; Society of the Cincinnati

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the Society of the Cincinnati — Revolutionary War officers' hereditary society.

narrative_blocks.header: First from Farmington to Boston — Noadiah led
narrative_blocks.body: At the Lexington alarm he volunteered in the company of his first cousin Capt. Noadiah Hooker — 111 men, the first company from Farmington and the first from Connecticut to reach the Continental Army at Boston. He entered as a private and rose through sergeant, ensign, and 2nd lieutenant to Major of the Continental Line.

narrative_blocks.header: Flag of truce — Burgoyne's surrender delivered
narrative_blocks.body: He was sent under a flag of truce to the British commander in New York to formally announce the surrender of Burgoyne's army in October 1777. He also helped fit out fire ships at New York in 1776 under Washington's plan to attack British vessels. EH records he was "throughout the war held in high esteem by Gen. Washington, and frequently entrusted with responsible and important duties."

narrative_blocks.header: West Indies mariner — then Sheriff until 70
narrative_blocks.body: Before the Revolution he made eleven voyages to the West Indies as a merchant mariner. After the war he became Sheriff of Hartford County, served as Justice of the Peace until age 70, and was a Farmington selectman. He disposed of his estate to his brother-in-law before his death, retaining possession during his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife was the Governor's sister, not daughter
narrative_blocks.body: EH called Mary Treadwell the "daughter" of Gov. John Treadwell — impossible, as Treadwell was born 1745 and Mary in 1755, making him only ten at her birth. Ephraim Treadwell's 1781 will names both "daughter Mary" and "son John Treadwell" as executor, confirming they were siblings.

narrative_blocks.header: Purchased Tunxis land with Elnathan in 1776
narrative_blocks.body: In 1776, he and his brother Elnathan purchased 40 acres at Congee, Farmington — including a dwelling house and barn — from Tunxis men Cornelius Indian and Elijah Wampey for £150. The deed is preserved at the Native Northeast Portal as one of the documented transfers of Native land in the Farmington area.

narrative_blocks.header: Cincinnati member — half-brother Thomas died in war
narrative_blocks.body: He and his brother-in-law Capt. Simeon Newell were both original members of the Society of the Cincinnati, founded 1783 by Continental officers. His half-brother Thomas Hart Hooker (different mother, Mercy Hart) died of pleurisy during the same Revolution — at Roxbury in 1775, age 30 — never seeing its end.

military_service.unit: Company of Capt. Noadiah Hooker; Continental Line (eventually Major)
military_service.notes: Enlisted at first call (Lexington alarm) in company of his cousin Capt. Noadiah Hooker — 111 men, first from Farmington and first from Connecticut to reach the army at Boston. Rose: Private → Sergeant → Ensign → 2nd Lieutenant (under Washington) → Major. 1776: Fitted out fire ships at New York to attack British vessels (Washington's plan). As brigade major: managed correspondence for generals on whose staff he served. Sent under flag of truce to British commander in New York to announce Burgoyne's surrender. "Throughout the war held in high esteem by Gen. Washington, and frequently entrusted with responsible and important duties." Member, Society of the Cincinnati.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband and half-brother both Cincinnati members
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Capt. Simeon Newell and her half-brother Maj. Roger Hooker both served as Continental officers and were original members of the Society of the Cincinnati. After the war, both families moved from Connecticut to western New York — Simeon dying at Sodus Bay in 1813, Mercy following in 1817 at Wolcott.

burial.plot_notes: Gravestone confirmed

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Isaac Cowles

burial.plot_notes: Bolton Center Cemetery, Bolton, Tolland County, Connecticut. Cenotaphic inscription also on Olcott family monument in West Cemetery, Manchester CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Yale-educated 'quaint and erratic' preacher
narrative_blocks.body: Cynthia married Rev. Allen Olcott in 1792 — a Yale College 1768 graduate settled at the Farmington Presbyterian Church in 1787. After four years his preaching was deemed 'quaint and erratic' enough that he was dismissed and never settled at any other church.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled with Allen on a South Manchester farm
narrative_blocks.body: After his Farmington dismissal Allen returned to his native East Hartford and then settled with Cynthia on a farm at South Manchester, Connecticut. Family stories suggest his farming methods were as unique as his preaching had been. He died there in April 1811, leaving Cynthia a widow with five children ranging from eight to eighteen.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried Capt. Samuel Alvord at fifty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Two years after Allen's death Cynthia remarried — Capt. Samuel Alvord of East Hartford, on 14 January 1813. The couple lived at East Hartford until her own death in June 1827. Through her first marriage's daughter Cynthia, her line married back into the Talcott-Hooker convergence.

burial.plot_notes: No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

career.notes: Succeeded brother-in-law Roger Newton

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

burial.plot_notes: Exact location unknown; earliest surviving identifiable gravestone in cemetery dates to 1648 Memorial plaque text: "Thomas Hooker 1586-1647. A leader of the founders in this commonwealth. A preacher of persuasive power. A statesman who based all civil authority on the free consent of the people."

marriages.officiant: Rev. Robert Chaloner, D.D., rector of Amersham (Susanna's aunt's husband)

sources.full: J.R. Hutchinson, 'The Rev. Thomas Hooker—An Alternative Descent,' NY G&B Record 48 (1917), p. 393
Josephine C. Frost, NY G&B Record 64 (Jan 1933), p. 2

narrative_blocks.header: Led 100 settlers from Cambridge to found Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: In the summer of 1636, Hooker led a company of one hundred men, women, and children from Newtown (Cambridge) to found Hartford — a hundred miles through wilderness with no path, guided only by a compass. They drove 160 cattle before them, cleared thickets with axes, built causeways from felled trees, rested in shady groves, and ate wild strawberries. They made the journey in a fortnight.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1638 sermon framed Connecticut's constitution
narrative_blocks.body: On May 31, 1638, preaching before the General Court assembled to frame Connecticut's constitution, Hooker declared: "The foundation of authority is laid in the free consent of the people." The Fundamental Orders of Connecticut, adopted January 14, 1639 — often called the world's first written democratic constitution — grew from this sermon.

narrative_blocks.header: Called the light of the western churches
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker was universally regarded by his contemporaries as the foremost preacher in New England — Cotton Mather called him "the light of the western churches."

narrative_blocks.header: He moderated the Hutchinson trial, then left
narrative_blocks.body: In 1637, Hooker served as one of two moderators over the three-week inquiry into Anne Hutchinson's antinomian doctrines — which culminated in her excommunication and banishment from Massachusetts Bay. Having completed his role, Hooker returned to Hartford before the trial concluded.

narrative_blocks.header: No portrait exists — only his descendants' faces
narrative_blocks.body: There is no known portrait of Thomas Hooker. The statue that stands before the Old State House in Hartford — near the site of the First Meeting House — was sculpted by comparing the likenesses of his descendants.

quotes.text: His genius would still haunt all the pulpits. He had great popularity and influence, especially with young ministers, to whom he was an oracle, and their principle library.

Having gone on, with much strength of voice and intention of spirit, about a quarter of an hour, he was at a stand and told the people, that God had deprived him of both his strength and matter, and so went forth and about half an hour after, returned again, and went on to very good purpose about tw
I giue vnto my sonne Samuell, in case the whole library come not to him... the summe of seuenty pounds.
Brother, I am going to receive mercy!
quotes.attribution: Thomas Hooker, deathbed, July 7, 1647 (Cotton Mather, Magnalia Christi Americana)

career.notes: Cared for Mrs. Joanna Drake; 'had his diet and lodging' in Drake household
Silenced by Bishop Laud for nonconformity

Congregation had preceded him
Founded with migration from Newtowne

education.notes: B.A. 1608; M.A. 1611; Fellow 1611-c.1618 — Entered divinity course; left before B.D.

narrative_blocks.header: They met in the same household in Surrey
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna Garbrand was working as a waiting-gentlewoman to Mrs. Joanna Drake at Esher, Surrey, when Thomas Hooker arrived in 1620 as curate of Esher church — "having his diet and lodging" in the Drake household. They married at Amersham on April 3, 1621, the ceremony performed by Susanna's own uncle by marriage, Rev. Robert Chaloner.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Oxford's Reformation bookseller
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna descended from the Garbrands, a Calvinist family who fled the Low Countries for England. Her grandfather Garbrand Harks became the foremost Oxford bookseller of the Reformation and official stationer to the University, a post the family held for three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed on the Griffin in the great migration
narrative_blocks.body: In 1633 Susanna sailed to New England aboard the Griffin with her husband and children, part of the Puritan migration that brought some twenty thousand settlers before 1640. The family settled first at Cambridge, then helped found Hartford in 1636.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried Elder William Goodwin after Hooker's death
narrative_blocks.body: After Thomas Hooker died in 1647, Susanna married Elder William Goodwin, a leading Hartford churchman. In 1654 a Hartford court punished Walter Gray for slandering the couple with a charge of adultery.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived Hooker by nearly thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna outlived Thomas Hooker by almost thirty years, dying at Farmington in 1676 at about eighty-three. Through her eight children the Hooker line descended to figures including the financier J.P. Morgan and President William Howard Taft.

burial.plot_notes: Marker: rough stone with rude inscription. Marker present.

notable.notable_blurb: First Mayor of New York City, 1665

narrative_blocks.header: From Plymouth Pilgrim to New York's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City by Governor Richard Nicolls in 1665, shortly after the English took the city from the Dutch. He had joined the English expedition against New Amsterdam the previous year alongside Governor Winthrop of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded Miles Standish as Plymouth's military captain
narrative_blocks.body: A trusted Plymouth Colony leader, Willet succeeded the legendary Miles Standish as Captain of the Plymouth military company in 1648. He had arrived in Plymouth on the ship Lion in 1632, associated with the Leyden congregation.

narrative_blocks.header: He bought Attleboro from Massasoit's son
narrative_blocks.body: As a Plymouth trader, Willett negotiated the Rehoboth North Purchase, acquiring the lands that became Attleboro and North Attleboro. He bought them from Wamsutta, a son of the Wampanoag sachem Massasoit.

narrative_blocks.header: He brokered New Amsterdam's surrender
narrative_blocks.body: In 1664 Willett joined the English expedition under Richard Nicolls and helped arrange the peaceful surrender of Dutch New Amsterdam. When the city was renamed New York, he became its first mayor the next year.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Plymouth's Major John Brown
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Browne was a daughter of Major John Brown, a leading citizen of Plymouth Colony. In 1636 she married the trader Thomas Willett, who would become New York's first mayor.

narrative_blocks.header: She bore fourteen children across two decades
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Browne and Thomas Willett had fourteen children over more than twenty years at Plymouth. She died early in 1670, and her husband remarried the following year.

burialCemetery.name: Memento Mori Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Rev. Samuel Hooker buried here; no stone survives.
